title: State of Managed Kubernetes 2020
question: Summarize the key point of 'State of Managed Kubernetes 2020' in one concise sentence.
expected_answer: The article compares managed Kubernetes offerings (EKS, AKS, GKE) and emphasizes that feature parity has increased, with GKE still strong on automation but others catching up, affecting developer choice.
evidence_quote: the most notable difference from 2019 to now has been the feature parity across the clouds.
actual_answer:
By 2020, managed Kubernetes adoption had surged and major cloud providers' offerings had reached near feature parity, making developer-facing trade-offs (e.g., EKS's flexibility but greater management burden) the main differentiator. 

I draw this from the article noting that "Kubernetes usage in production jumped from 58% to 78%" (CNCF survey) and that "the most notable difference from 2019 to now has been the feature parity across the clouds. The huge lead that GKE once enjoyed has been largely reduced," while EKS "provides a large degree of flexibility ... [but] the management burden falls on the developer."

title: How we initiate point of sale transactions globally
question: Summarize the main idea of 'How we initiate point of sale transactions globally' in one short sentence.
expected_answer: The article explains Adyen’s Terminal API approach—using Nexo protocol and WebSockets—to avoid embedded libraries and enable reliable bidirectional, cloud-routable POS communication.
evidence_quote: we removed our need for libraries and created the Terminal API, adopting the Nexo protocol — a card payment standard that facilitates communication between the cash register and terminal.
actual_answer:
They replaced embedded device libraries with a JSON-based Terminal API (using the Nexo protocol) and cloud/WebSocket routing so merchants can initiate POS payments centrally without complex per-store network setups.

This is based on the article’s description that “we removed our need for libraries and created the Terminal API, adopting the Nexo protocol,” which uses “request/response JSON messaging,” and that they “adapted our Terminal API for the cloud” so merchants can “initiate a terminal payment directly with Adyen’s backend,” removing the need for VPNs, fixed IPs, or port forwarding.

titles: How we initiate point of sale transactions globally
Artificial Intelligence Marketing
Platforms — from Steemit to OpenBazaar pt.1
Nimiq and Kindhumans
Why Do More Buying Choices Cause Unhappiness?
Selling Your New Sales Deck to Sales
Why Fintech Companies Use Haskell

evaluation.rationale: The model answer accurately summarizes the expected main idea: replacing embedded libraries with a JSON-based Terminal API using the Nexo protocol and cloud/WebSocket routing to enable central POS initiation and remove per-store network complexity; this is supported by the provided evidence.

title: Transfer File From FTP Server to AWS S3 Bucket Using Python
question: I need a concise, practical tutorial to move files from an FTP server into an S3 bucket using Python — which article should I read from the dataset and why (one short justification with a supporting quote)?
expected_answer: Read 'Transfer File From FTP Server to AWS S3 Bucket Using Python' (article_id: 109) because it provides step-by-step functions and lists the required Python libraries (paramiko, boto3).
evidence_quote: pip install paramiko boto3
actual_answer:
Read: "Transfer File From FTP Server to AWS S3 Bucket Using Python" by Kiran Kumbhar — https://medium.com/better-programming/transfer-file-from-ftp-server-to-a-s3-bucket-using-python-7f9e51f44e35

Justification (short): It’s a practical, step‑by‑step Python tutorial using the exact libraries you need (paramiko and boto3) and shows a concrete transfer function. Supporting quote: "In this article we will implement file transfer (from ftp server to amazon s3) functionality in python using the paramiko and boto3 modules" and "The transfer_file_from_ftp_to_s3() function takes a bunch of arguments... The program reads the file from the ftp path and copies the same file to S3 bucket at the given s3 path."

title: Increase GlusterFS volume size in Kubernetes
question: Which article would you recommend to a Kubernetes admin who needs to expand persistent storage in-cluster, and give one supporting excerpt?
expected_answer: Recommend 'Increase GlusterFS volume size in Kubernetes' (article_id: 48) because it shows two practical methods to increase volume capacity and step-by-step Heketi commands.

actual_answer:
Recommend: "Increase GlusterFS volume size in Kubernetes" by Aymen Lamara (https://medium.com/wescale/increase-glusterfs-volume-size-in-kubernetes-752e087bf065).

Supporting excerpt (from the article passage): "One of the common issues when using a persistent volume solution in Kubernetes, like GlusterFS, is to start running out of storage capacity in your cluster, and you have to find a smooth way to increase volume size without losing your data. In this short article I will show you two ways of doing it."

title: User Experience Design Process
question: Which article should a product manager read to get an accessible overview of the UX design process and why (support with a short quote)?
expected_answer: Recommend 'User Experience Design Process' (article_id: 259) because it defines UX and outlines a six-stage iterative process centered on understanding users and improving usability.
evidence_quote: User Experience Design (UXD or UED) is the process of enhancing user satisfaction with a product by improving the usability, accessibility, and pleasure provided in the interaction with the product.
actual_answer: Read "User Experience Design Process" by Saadia Minhas. It gives a clear, stage-by-stage overview of UX work and explicitly includes the Product Manager among the stakeholders a PM would work with — the article explains that the "User experience design process is an iterative method that helps you continuously improve and polish your designs." This makes it an accessible primer for a product manager.
